title: 2025-2026 Respiratory Disease Season Outlook - December Update | CFA: Qualitative Assessments | CDC
text:
, it appears uptake may be higher compared to this time in 2024: as of October 2025 , among females with an infant born since April 1, 39.6% of infants were reported to be protected against RSV through either maternal vaccination (3.3%) or receipt of monoclonal antibody (36.3%), compared to 30.1% of infants protected in October of last year . 
RSV vaccination eligibility has expanded this year to include more high-risk adults. RSV vaccines are now recommended for all adults age 75 and older, and adults age 50 and older who are at high risk for severe RSV disease due to certain underlying medical or immunocompromising conditions. CDC modeling indicates that 17% of RSV hospitalizations in all adults over 50 years could be prevented if vaccine coverage remains similar to coverage in the 2024-2025 season compared to a scenario in which no adults were ever vaccinated. Among adults 75+, the same modeling shows vaccination could avert 23% of all hospitalizations due to RSV. As of December 6 , 41.2% of adults 75 years or older have received the RSV vaccine since it was first available during the 2023-2024 respiratory virus season, while an additional 9.2% indicate that they definitely will receive the RSV vaccine this year. This is similar to rates last year , when 40.5% of adults 75 years or older reported that they had ever received the RSV vaccine, as of November 23, 2024.
